agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand signals. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague — a vision statement, not a specification. Many critical business rules are implied but undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined measure of customer/market request volume for delivery services — no specification of how it is measured, what signals constitute it, or what granularity (zone, city, time window)" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — CEO says 'probably', indicating this is not yet confirmed as a hard requirement" },
      { "term": "Real time", "definition": "Price adjustments happen dynamically as demand changes, not on a batch/scheduled basis — exact latency tolerance undefined" },
      { "term": "Base price", "definition": "Implied but never stated — a reference price from which adjustments are made. Without this concept, 'prices go up/down' has no anchor" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No manual intervention required for price adjustments once the system is running — implies no human-in-the-loop approval for individual price changes" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy — the direction of the pricing curve is a strategic choice. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during high demand for a promotional campaign. The correlation between demand and price is policy, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds the 'high' threshold, the calculated price must be greater than the base price for the same delivery parameters"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same reasoning as BR-1 — lowering prices during low demand is a competitive strategy, not a system integrity constraint. Policy could change to maintain a floor price.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand falls below the 'low' threshold, the calculated price must be less than the base price for the same delivery parameters"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — not batch or deferred",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time vs near-real-time vs batch is an operational policy decision. The system is not corrupt if a price update takes 30 seconds instead of 1 second. Latency tolerance is undefined.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand signal, the system must recalculate and publish a new price within [undefined SLA — needs specification]"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual approval step for individual changes",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is an operational policy. Many dynamic pricing systems start automated and add manual overrides or circuit breakers. A VP could mandate human approval above certain thresholds.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a demand signal change, the system must calculate and apply a new price without requiring human intervention"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must prevent prices from reaching extreme values (both ceiling and floor)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Guardrails are explicitly described as 'probably' — not even confirmed as a requirement yet. Even if implemented, the specific bounds are pure policy. However, NOTE: if guardrails ARE implemented, the enforcement of those bounds would be invariant (see BR-6).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any demand level, the calculated price must remain within configured min/max bounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "If price guardrails are configured, no published price may ever violate them — the system must clamp or reject, never publish an out-of-bounds price",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Once guardrails exist, violating them is a data integrity issue. A price outside configured bounds reaching a customer is a corrupt state — it could constitute price gouging (legal risk) or selling below cost. The guardrail values themselves are policy; their enforcement is invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given guardrails are configured with min=X and max=Y, no price returned by the system may be < X or > Y under any demand condition"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and commercially nonsensical — you cannot pay a customer to deliver their package in a standard pricing model. This is a fundamental domain constraint regardless of policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any demand level and any guardrail configuration, the final published price must be > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The system must maintain profitability — prices should not systematically fall below cost",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Profitability is a business objective, not a system invariant. Companies routinely price below cost for market share, promotions, or strategic reasons. The CEO says 'competitive but also profitable' — this is a goal, not a hard constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given cost data is available, the average price over a defined period should exceed the average cost of delivery — exact margin threshold undefined"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The pricing system must have a defined base/reference price from which adjustments are calculated",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without a base price, 'go up' and 'go down' are meaningless — there is no anchor. The system cannot function without a reference point. This is a structural invariant of any adjustment-based pricing model.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price calculation must reference a base price; the system must reject or error if no base price is available for a given delivery"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is exactly at the boundary between 'high' and 'normal' or 'low' and 'normal'", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — needs demand threshold specification. Risk of price oscillation/flickering at boundaries." },
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., flash sale, weather event)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — should there be a rate-of-change limit on price increases to avoid customer shock? CEO's 'guardrails' may or may not cover this." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price should decrease but must remain above zero (BR-7) and above any configured floor (BR-6). Exact floor value undefined." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple overlapping demand zones with different demand levels", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — granularity of demand measurement (per zone, per city, per route) is not specified." },
    { "scenario": "System loses access to demand data feed", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — should it hold last known price, revert to base price, or halt quoting? Critical failure mode not addressed." },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price, but demand shifts before they confirm — which price do they pay?", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — price lock/quote expiry policy is not specified. This is a major UX and trust concern." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail configuration is missing or misconfigured (e.g., min > max)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configuration and refuse to operate with corrupt bounds (BR-6 enforcement)." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws triggered during emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging statutes. The system may be legally required to cap prices during declared emergencies regardless of demand." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-25% in high-demand periods but risks customer churn if perceived as exploitative. Without proper guardrails, extreme prices could trigger regulatory fines or legal action in jurisdictions with price gouging laws.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with low-latency requirements, and monitoring/alerting for price anomalies. 'Fully automated' removes human bottleneck but also removes human judgment — operational risk if the algorithm misbehaves.",
    "user": "Significant customer experience impact. Transparent dynamic pricing (like ride-sharing surge) can be accepted if communicated clearly. Hidden or erratic pricing destroys trust. Price volatility at checkout (quote expiry) is a major friction point.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Legal/regulatory risk from price gouging laws, reputational risk from perceived unfairness, financial risk from algorithm errors publishing extreme prices. The vagueness of 'probably have guardrails' is itself a risk — guardrails should be non-negotiable.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Pay variable prices — need transparency, fairness perception, and price stability at checkout" },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Demand signals must feed pricing system — coupling between dispatch and pricing" },
      { "group": "Finance", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes more complex with variable pricing; margin analysis requires demand correlation" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review price gouging regulations per jurisdiction; automated pricing may require audit trails" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability; need policies for price disputes and refunds" },
      { "group": "Sales/Partnerships", "impact": "Contract customers and partners may need fixed-price agreements exempt from dynamic pricing" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's statement is a vision pitch, not a specification. Almost every critical detail is undefined: demand measurement methodology, price adjustment algorithm, guardrail values, latency SLA, geographic granularity, quote locking policy, regulatory compliance approach, and failure modes. The word 'probably' applied to guardrails — the single most important safety feature — demonstrates this is pre-specification. I can extract directional intent but cannot define acceptance criteria with any confidence.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices increase with high demand (directional intent is clear)",
      "Prices decrease with low demand (directional intent is clear)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Positive price invariant (domain fundamental)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds defined",
      "Whether guardrails are mandatory or optional ('probably')",
      "Specific guardrail values (min/max prices)",
      "Demand signal definition and measurement granularity",
      "Real-time latency SLA",
      "Price quote locking and expiry policy",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements by jurisdiction",
      "Failure mode behavior when demand data is unavailable",
      "Whether contract/enterprise customers are subject to dynamic pricing",
      "Rate-of-change limits on price adjustments"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base/reference price exists from which adjustments are made — implied but never stated",
      "Demand is measurable in real time via some signal (order volume, quote requests, etc.) — mechanism undefined",
      "Guardrails, despite 'probably', are necessary for legal and operational safety",
      "Dynamic pricing applies to all delivery types — no exclusions mentioned",
      "The system operates per geographic zone — granularity assumed but not specified"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrails are described as 'probably' — this must be resolved as mandatory or optional before any implementation. Without confirmed guardrails, the system poses legal and reputational risk from unbounded price surges.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Guardrails are mandatory with defined min/max", "Guardrails are optional and configurable per market", "Guardrails are mandatory but values are market-specific"],
      "recommendation": "Guardrails must be mandatory. Recommend requiring Legal/Compliance sign-off on maximum price multiplier before launch."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of 'demand' — what signal measures it, at what granularity (time window, geography, service type), and what thresholds constitute 'high' vs 'low'. This is the core input to the entire system and is completely undefined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations/Dispatch", "Finance", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Order volume per zone per hour", "Quote request rate vs courier availability ratio", "Composite signal combining volume, capacity, and external factors"],
      "recommendation": "Demand should be defined as the ratio of active delivery requests to available courier capacity within a geographic zone and time window. This is the standard logistics industry approach."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No price quote locking policy defined. If a customer sees a price and demand shifts before they confirm, which price do they pay? This is a critical UX and trust issue.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Price locked at quote time for N minutes", "Price always reflects current demand at confirmation time", "Price locked but with a cap on how much it can increase"],
      "recommendation": "Price should be locked at quote time for a defined window (e.g., 5-10 minutes). Confirming a different price than displayed destroys customer trust."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory compliance framework mentioned. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging laws that cap price increases during declared emergencies. A fully automated system could violate these laws.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": ["Build emergency override that disables dynamic pricing", "Integrate with emergency declaration feeds to auto-cap prices", "Limit to jurisdictions without price gouging laws initially"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review is mandatory before launch. System must support emergency price caps per jurisdiction."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No failure mode defined — what happens when demand data is unavailable or stale? A 'fully automated' system with no fallback and no human-in-the-loop could either freeze prices at stale values, revert to base, or stop quoting entirely.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations/Dispatch", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Revert to base price when demand data is stale beyond threshold", "Hold last known price for a decay period then revert to base", "Halt quoting and surface error to customer"],
      "recommendation": "Revert to base price when demand signal is stale beyond a configurable threshold. Never halt quoting — that stops revenue."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
